h1Title: Peripheral Artery Disease: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Peripheral artery disease (PAD) affects the arteries supplying the legs — and less commonly the arms — when atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks arterial flow below the aortic bifurcation. PAD affects approximately 8.5 million US adults over age 40 and 202 million people globally, according to the 2019 Global Burden of Disease study. The hallmark symptom is intermittent claudication — reproducible calf, thigh, or buttock pain during walking that resolves within minutes of rest. Approximately 30% of patients have no leg symptoms, diagnosed only by a low ankle-brachial index (ABI). Severe disease — critical limb-threatening ischaemia (CLTI) — causes rest pain, ulceration, and gangrene, carrying a 20-30% risk of major amputation within one year. Treatment spans medical management with statins and antiplatelet agents, supervised exercise rehabilitation, and endovascular or surgical revascularisation.

keyStats.prevalence: 8.5 million US adults over 40 affected (NHANES); 202 million worldwide (GBD 2019); prevalence rises to 15-20% in those over 70
keyStats.demographics: Men affected slightly more than women at younger ages; female prevalence equalises after age 70. Black Americans have twice the prevalence of White Americans after adjustment for conventional risk factors.
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic claudication typically presents age 60-70; CLTI typically 70+ years; PAD in diabetic patients often presents a decade earlier
keyStats.globalCases: 202 million people worldwide with PAD (GBD 2019); PAD accounts for over 100,000 major amputations per year in the United States

definition: Peripheral artery disease (ICD-10: I70.2–I70.9) is atherosclerotic obstruction of the arteries of the lower extremities — principally the aortoiliac, femoropopliteal, and tibioperoneal segments — resulting in chronic limb ischaemia. The same pathological process that drives coronary and carotid atherosclerosis produces plaque build-up in leg arteries, reducing perfusion reserve and, in severe cases, resting perfusion. PAD severity is classified by the Fontaine system (I–IV) or the more granular Rutherford classification (0–6), both grading from asymptomatic (Fontaine I) through claudication (II) to rest pain (III) and tissue loss (IV). The recently introduced chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (CLTI) concept replaces the older term critical limb ischaemia and incorporates wound, ischaemia, and foot infection (WIfI) staging to predict amputation risk more accurately. PAD is a systemic marker of polyvascular atherosclerosis: 40-60% of PAD patients have concurrent significant coronary artery disease, and PAD independently predicts 2-3-fold excess cardiovascular mortality. Management requires vascular surgeons, interventional radiologists, cardiologists, diabetologists, and wound-care specialists working as a multidisciplinary limb-preservation team.

typesClassification.description: Detectable only by a low ankle-brachial index (ABI below 0.9); no leg symptoms. Accounts for approximately 50% of PAD cases. Still carries elevated cardiovascular event risk; statin and antiplatelet therapy indicated.

Reproducible muscle pain, cramping, or fatigue in the calf, thigh, or buttock during a defined walking distance, resolving within 1-5 minutes of rest. Caused by insufficient arterial flow to meet muscular oxygen demand under exercise conditions.

Constant burning pain in the forefoot and toes at rest, worsened by leg elevation (e.g. in bed) and partially relieved by hanging the foot dependent. Indicates severely reduced baseline perfusion (ABI typically below 0.4 or toe pressure below 30 mmHg).

typesClassification.type: Tissue loss and gangrene (Fontaine IV / CLTI)
typesClassification.description: Non-healing arterial ulcers, digital gangrene, or more extensive wet gangrene. Without revascularisation, 30% of CLTI patients require major amputation within one year. Prognosis for life and limb is poor: 1-year mortality approximately 25%.

Sudden arterial occlusion — from thrombosis or embolism — producing the '6Ps': pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, and poikilothermia. A surgical emergency with 6-hour window to avoid irreversible muscle death.

primarySymptoms: Intermittent claudication — reproducible calf, thigh, or buttock cramping or fatigue triggered by a consistent walking distance and resolving within 5 minutes of standing still; the most common initial presentation of PAD.

Rest pain — constant, often burning or aching pain in the foot and toes at night or when the leg is elevated; relieved by hanging the foot over the edge of the bed or standing; indicates critical ischaemia.
Non-healing wounds or ulcers on the foot or ankle, especially over bony prominences, that fail to show healing progress despite standard wound care.
Digital gangrene — blackening and desiccation of one or more toes, sometimes beginning as persistent bluish or mottled discolouration.
Cold feet or legs, particularly noticeable unilaterally or compared with upper extremities, from reduced arterial inflow.
Reduced or absent foot and ankle pulses (posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries) detected on physical examination.
Skin changes of chronic ischaemia — hair loss on the lower leg, shiny thin skin, pallor on elevation and dependent rubor (Buerger's sign).
Erectile dysfunction from aortoiliac occlusive disease (Leriche syndrome) affecting internal iliac artery perfusion, accompanied by bilateral buttock claudication and absent femoral pulses.
Sudden severe leg pain, pallor, and pulselessness indicating acute limb ischaemia from in-situ thrombosis or peripheral embolism — a vascular emergency.
earlyWarningSigns: Calf pain that comes on reliably at the same walking distance and disappears with a brief rest — even if mild, this is claudication until proven otherwise.
Ankle-brachial index below 0.9 on routine vascular screening in a diabetic or hypertensive patient, even without leg symptoms.
Slow-healing minor foot wound or blister in a diabetic patient with poor pedal pulses — tissue loss can accelerate rapidly.
Night pain in the foot that wakes the patient and is relieved by dangling the leg — a transition signal from claudication to rest pain.
emergencySigns: Acute limb ischaemia — sudden severe leg pain, pallor, pulselessness, coldness, and sensory or motor loss — a 4-6-hour window to revascularise before irreversible muscle loss; call emergency services immediately.
Rapidly spreading wet gangrene with systemic sepsis (fever, tachycardia, confusion) — requires urgent surgical debridement and vascular consultation to control life-threatening infection.
Haemorrhage from a ruptured anastomosis or pseudoaneurysm after prior bypass surgery — presents as expanding groin or leg haematoma; call emergency services.
Sudden absent pulse in a previously functioning bypass graft — acute graft thrombosis requiring emergency thrombectomy or thrombolysis.

causes.cause: Atherosclerosis — the dominant cause in over 95% of cases
causes.description: The same LDL-driven plaque accumulation that narrows coronary and carotid arteries progressively stenoses the aortoiliac, superficial femoral, popliteal, and tibial arteries. The superficial femoral artery in the adductor canal is the most commonly and severely affected segment. Atherosclerosis in PAD is accelerated by the same risk factors as coronary disease: hypertension, dyslipidaemia, diabetes, and smoking.

causes.cause: Cigarette smoking — the strongest single modifiable risk factor for PAD
causes.description: Smokers have a 2-4-fold higher PAD incidence than non-smokers; the relative risk in current smokers is higher for PAD than for coronary artery disease. Smoking cessation is associated with improved walking distance, lower amputation rates, and better graft patency. Nicotine and carbon monoxide promote endothelial dysfunction, LDL oxidation, and platelet aggregation.

Diabetes confers a 3-4-fold excess PAD risk and a dramatically different disease pattern — more tibial and peroneal vessel disease with relative sparing of larger vessels, producing a distinctive distal ischaemic pattern and much higher rates of foot ulceration and amputation. Every 1% rise in HbA1c is associated with a 28% increase in PAD risk.

Hypertension approximately doubles PAD risk through accelerated atherosclerosis and endothelial shear stress. Blood pressure control to below 130/80 mmHg reduces cardiovascular events in PAD patients but does not directly reverse claudication; ankle-brachial index typically does not improve with BP reduction alone.

Acute emboli from atrial fibrillation, left ventricular thrombus post-MI, or aortic aneurysm can acutely occlude peripheral arteries without underlying chronic PAD, producing acute limb ischaemia. Paradoxically, some patients with PAD develop acute-on-chronic occlusion when an atherosclerotic plaque ruptures in situ.

Buerger's disease (thromboangiitis obliterans) affects young male heavy smokers, producing inflammatory occlusion of small and medium arteries in hands and feet. Popliteal artery entrapment affects young athletes. Fibromuscular dysplasia and vasculitis (e.g., Takayasu arteritis) account for rare cases. These conditions are clinically important because they require different treatment algorithms.

riskFactors.description: The most powerful PAD risk factor: relative risk 2-4 in current smokers. Duration and pack-years are dose-dependent risk determinants. Cessation reduces PAD progression, improves bypass graft patency, and is the single most impactful intervention.

Triples or quadruples PAD risk; the largest attributable risk factor in South and East Asian populations. Glycaemic control (HbA1c target below 7%) combined with foot surveillance is the cornerstone of PAD prevention in diabetes.

Doubles PAD risk; present in approximately 50% of PAD patients. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred in PAD because they reduce cardiovascular events and improve walking time independently of BP effects.

LDL-C above 130 mg/dL significantly accelerates PAD progression. High-intensity statin therapy reduces the ankle-brachial index decline rate and lowers major adverse limb events (MALE) by approximately 18% in meta-analyses.

Prevalence roughly doubles per decade after age 40; approximately 15-20% of those over 70 have detectable PAD on ABI screening. Age-related arterial stiffness and plaque burden drive this relationship.
CKD triples PAD prevalence; calcific medial arteriopathy in uraemia can produce an artificially elevated ABI despite significant ischaemia. Toe-brachial index is more accurate in these patients.

Black Americans have roughly 2× the prevalence of White Americans after adjustment for traditional risk factors; higher rates of hypertension and diabetes partly explain but do not fully account for this disparity.

affectedDemographics: US adults over 40: 8.5 million with PAD (NHANES prevalence data). Prevalence 4-5% aged 40-59, rising to 15-20% aged 70+.
Black Americans: approximately twice the PAD prevalence and significantly higher amputation rates than White Americans despite similar symptomatic presentation rates.
Diabetics: 3-4× PAD risk; PAD presents a decade earlier in diabetic patients and predominantly affects tibial and small-vessel distribution, causing high rates of foot ulceration.
Women: often underdiagnosed because claudication may be less typical; women with PAD have at least equivalent cardiovascular mortality to men and higher rates of functional limitation.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of PAD follows the 2016 AHA/ACC PAD Guideline and begins with a structured history for claudication symptoms, combined with a focused vascular examination: inspection of the legs for skin changes, ulcers, and gangrene; palpation of femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis pulses; and auscultation for femoral bruits. The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is the cornerstone non-invasive test: a ratio of ankle systolic blood pressure to brachial systolic blood pressure measured with a Doppler probe and sphygmomanometer. An ABI at rest of 0.90 or below diagnoses PAD with 79% sensitivity and 96% specificity compared with arteriography. An ABI above 1.3 suggests non-compressible calcified vessels (common in diabetes and CKD) and requires toe-brachial index or waveform analysis instead. Exercise ABI — performed before and after treadmill walking — detects haemodynamically significant disease in patients with a borderline resting ABI of 0.9-1.0. Duplex ultrasound characterises the location and severity of stenosis non-invasively before intervention planning. CT angiography or MR angiography provide a road map for revascularisation, delineating inflow and outflow vessels from the aorta to the foot. Conventional catheter-based digital subtraction angiography is reserved for the planning or conduct of endovascular intervention. In critical limb ischaemia, toe pressure below 30 mmHg or transcutaneous oxygen pressure (TcPO2) below 30 mmHg at the wound site predicts poor healing without revascularisation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for and diagnoses PAD; an ABI at rest ≤0.90 confirms PAD with high specificity; severity classification guides management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute non-invasive test in a vascular lab or clinic. Lie flat while blood pressure cuffs are placed on both arms and ankles; a hand-held Doppler probe detects flow sounds. No injection or radiation. ABI below 0.9 is diagnostic; below 0.4 indicates severe disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects haemodynamically significant PAD not apparent on resting ABI (0.9-1.0) by measuring ABI immediately after standardised walking
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Walk on a treadmill at a standardised gradient and speed until symptom onset or a maximum of 5 minutes. Blood pressures are repeated within 1-2 minutes of stopping. A post-exercise ABI drop of more than 20% confirms exercise-induced ischaemia.

diagnosticTests.test: Duplex ultrasound of lower limb arteries
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive characterisation of stenosis location, severity, and plaque morphology from the aortoiliac to tibial segments
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45-60 minute gel-based ultrasound scan with a Doppler probe over each arterial segment from the groin to the ankle. Peak systolic velocity ratios above 2.5 indicate 50-75% stenosis. No radiation or contrast.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography (CTA) of the aorta and lower limbs
diagnosticTests.purpose: High-resolution anatomic road map of arterial disease extent and severity from the aortic bifurcation to pedal vessels; used for pre-intervention planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-minute CT scan; iodinated contrast injected via an arm cannula. Covers from the renal arteries to the feet. Excellent resolution of calcified and non-calcified plaque; allows measurement of stenosis length for stent or bypass planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides similar road map to CTA without ionising radiation; preferred in patients with renal impairment when gadolinium rather than iodinated contrast is used
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner; a gadolinium contrast agent is injected. Contraindicated in patients with certain pacemakers or metallic implants. Image quality may be limited by patient movement or calcification.

diagnosticTests.test: Toe-brachial index (TBI) and transcutaneous oxygen pressure (TcPO2)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses PAD in patients with non-compressible arteries (ABI > 1.3 in diabetes/CKD); TcPO2 below 30 mmHg predicts poor wound healing and high amputation risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: TBI: a small cuff on the great toe and Doppler probe; non-invasive. TcPO2: small electrode patches placed on the dorsum of the foot measure local tissue oxygen levels over 15-20 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows the 2016 ACC/AHA PAD Guideline and is staged according to disease severity and limb threat. Guideline-directed medical therapy is the foundation for all patients: high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg) to reduce cardiovascular events and slow PAD progression; single antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75 mg or clopidogrel 75 mg); ACE inhibitor or ARB; and aggressive risk-factor control. Smoking cessation is the most impactful behavioural intervention and nearly doubles the symptom-free walking distance at 6 months. Supervised exercise rehabilitation for claudication is a Class I recommendation in US and European guidelines, producing greater improvement in walking distance than revascularisation alone for mild-moderate claudication in RCTs; the recommended programme runs 30-60 minutes three times weekly for at least 12 weeks. When claudication is severely disabling despite 3 months of exercise and medical therapy, endovascular revascularisation is considered: balloon angioplasty, stenting, or atherectomy of the aortoiliac or femoropopliteal segments, depending on anatomy. Drug-coated balloons (DCBs) reduce restenosis in the femoropopliteal segment, with 1-year patency above 70%. For critical limb-threatening ischaemia, urgent revascularisation is required to prevent limb loss: endovascular approaches are preferred as the first attempt because of lower immediate morbidity, with surgical bypass reserved for endovascular failures or anatomy unsuitable for catheter-based treatment. The BEST-CLI trial demonstrated superiority of bypass surgery over endovascular intervention in patients with adequate saphenous vein conduit and extensive tibial disease.

medicalTreatments.name: High-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40-80 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces cardiovascular events in PAD patients by 22% per 38.7 mg/dL LDL-C reduction (CTT meta-analysis) and reduces major adverse limb events by approximately 18% in vascular-surgery cohort studies. All PAD patients regardless of baseline LDL-C receive high-intensity statin therapy per ACC/AHA and ESC guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 18% reduction in major adverse limb events; 22% reduction in major cardiovascular events per mmol/L LDL reduction

medicalTreatments.name: Single antiplatelet therapy — aspirin 75-100 mg/day or clopidogrel 75 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Clopidogrel is preferred over aspirin in symptomatic PAD based on CAPRIE trial data. Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily (COMPASS regimen) is now a Class IIa recommendation in guidelines for high-risk PAD with prior revascularisation, reducing MALE and MACE.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CAPRIE: clopidogrel reduced combined vascular events by 8.7% relative to aspirin in PAD subgroup (PMID 8918275). COMPASS: rivaroxaban + aspirin reduced MALE by 46% in PAD (PMID 29133020)

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitor or ARB (ramipril 10 mg/day, perindopril)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce cardiovascular events in PAD independently of blood pressure lowering. Ramipril 10 mg daily demonstrated a 22% relative risk reduction in major vascular events in the HOPE trial PAD subgroup. Also improve walking time by 60-80 metres in small randomised trials, possibly via nitric oxide pathway.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 22% relative risk reduction in cardiovascular events (HOPE PAD subgroup, PMID 10938172); modest improvement in walking distance

medicalTreatments.name: Cilostazol 100 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: A phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor that improves claudication walking distance through vasodilation and antiplatelet effects. FDA-approved specifically for claudication; contraindicated in heart failure. Provides approximately 50% improvement in maximum walking distance over 12-24 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analysis: 50% improvement in maximum walking distance vs. placebo; superior to pentoxifylline (PMID 11994405)

medicalTreatments.name: Supervised exercise rehabilitation (30-60 min, 3×/week, ≥12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: The most effective non-invasive intervention for claudication. Walking to near-maximal pain, resting, and repeating creates ischaemic preconditioning and promotes collateral formation. Cochrane meta-analysis shows 180% improvement in walking ability compared with baseline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2017 (PMID 28224631): walking distance improvement 100-180% vs. placebo; superior to angioplasty alone in mild-moderate claudication at 2 years

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular revascularisation — percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) with or without stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Balloon catheter crosses the stenosis or occlusion via femoral or popliteal access and dilates the narrowing under fluoroscopic guidance. Self-expanding nitinol stents are placed in the aortoiliac segment; drug-coated balloons are preferred in the femoropopliteal segment to reduce restenosis. Tibial angioplasty is used for CLTI with distal disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Aortoiliac stenting: 5-year primary patency 60-80%. Femoropopliteal drug-coated balloon: 1-year primary patency 70-80%. Tibial angioplasty: limb salvage in 70-80% of CLTI at 1 year.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical bypass grafting (femoro-popliteal and femoro-tibial bypass)
surgicalOptions.description: Prosthetic graft (PTFE or Dacron) or autologous saphenous vein is anastomosed to the femoral artery above and the popliteal or tibial artery below the obstruction. Vein bypass is strongly preferred for below-knee targets because of markedly superior patency. BEST-CLI trial confirmed vein bypass superiority over endovascular therapy in extensive tibial disease with adequate vein conduit.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vein bypass to popliteal: 5-year patency 70-80%; to tibial 50-60%. PTFE to popliteal: 5-year patency 50-60%. Limb salvage in CLTI: 80-85% at 1 year with patent graft.

surgicalOptions.name: Aortoiliac endarterectomy and aortobifemoral bypass for aortoiliac occlusive disease
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgical repair of aortoiliac occlusion (Leriche syndrome) with bilateral femoral grafting restores aortoiliac flow for bilateral claudication and impotence. Aortobifemoral bypass with Dacron Y-graft has 10-year patency above 85% and is preferred when endovascular options fail or anatomy is unfavourable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 10-year graft patency above 85%; 5-year claudication relief 80-90%; erectile function improves in 50-70% of men with Leriche syndrome.

surgicalOptions.name: Major amputation (below-knee or above-knee)
surgicalOptions.description: Last resort when revascularisation fails or is not feasible and gangrene or infection threatens life. Below-knee amputation preserves knee function and is associated with better rehabilitation outcomes than above-knee. Structured rehabilitation with prosthetic fitting and physiotherapy begins 4-6 weeks post-operatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality 5-10% for BKA; rehabilitation to functional prosthetic walking achieved in 50-70% of BKA patients under 70 without severe comorbidity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT, bupropion)
alternativeTreatments.description: Smoking cessation nearly doubles symptom-free walking distance at 6 months and significantly improves bypass graft patency. Varenicline plus counselling achieves 12-month quit rates of 25-35%. This is the single most effective lifestyle intervention in PAD and substantially modifies disease trajectory.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intermittent pneumatic compression therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: A wearable device that cyclically inflates around the foot and calf to augment arterial inflow by creating a diastolic pressure wave. Evidence for claudication improvement is moderate; most useful as an adjunct in CLTI patients awaiting revascularisation or unsuitable for surgery. NICE recommends it for non-reconstructable CLTI.

whySeeSpecialist: A vascular surgeon should be seen for any new claudication limiting daily activities, for an ankle-brachial index below 0.7, or for any rest pain, non-healing foot ulcer, or suspected acute limb ischaemia. A multidisciplinary foot and limb-preservation team is needed in diabetic PAD because foot infection complicates the vascular picture rapidly.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a vascular surgeon with documented high-volume practice in both endovascular and open bypass procedures, ideally at a centre with an integrated vascular interventional radiology team. For CLTI, seek a limb-preservation centre with podiatry, wound care, and orthotics services. Avoid centres that offer only amputation without a documented attempt at revascularisation.
hospitalCriteria: Hybrid operating theatre with fluoroscopy for combined endovascular and open surgery
Vascular lab with duplex ultrasound, ABI, and TcPO2 capability
Multidisciplinary limb-preservation team (vascular surgery, interventional radiology, podiatry, diabetology, wound care)
Vein-mapping ultrasound service for pre-operative conduit assessment
keyFacilities: Accredited vascular surgical centres with hybrid suites
Wound care clinics with hyperbaric oxygen option for refractory cases
Limb-preservation programmes (integrated vascular and foot teams)
Cardiac rehabilitation and structured exercise programmes
Prosthetics and orthotics services for post-amputation rehabilitation
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking: smoking cessation is the single most effective prevention strategy, cutting PAD incidence by 50-60% and markedly improving outcomes once disease is present.
Control blood sugar to HbA1c below 7% in diabetic patients — each 1% HbA1c reduction cuts PAD progression risk by 28%.
Achieve LDL-C below 100 mg/dL in intermediate-risk individuals and below 70 mg/dL in those with diabetes or established cardiovascular disease through dietary change and statin therapy.
Maintain blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.
Walk for at least 30 minutes daily — regular walking is both preventive and the primary treatment for established claudication.
Inspect feet daily for cuts, blisters, and pressure areas in diabetic patients; early detection prevents minor lesions progressing to major amputation.
lifestyleModifications: Walk to the point of moderate claudication pain, rest until it resolves, and repeat — daily structured walking creates collateral vessels and improves claudication distance by 100-180% over 12 weeks.
Stop smoking completely: within 6 months of cessation, claudication distance improves; bypass graft patency rates at 5 years are 30-40% higher in non-smokers.
Take statins, antiplatelet agents, and ACE inhibitors consistently; do not stop without specialist advice even if limb symptoms improve.
Inspect feet daily for blisters, cracks, ulcers, colour changes, or swelling; use a mirror for the sole; consult a podiatrist at the first sign of a wound.
Wear well-fitted, padded footwear — never go barefoot; use orthopaedic insoles if prescribed to redistribute pressure.
Control blood sugar carefully: even a small wound can progress to an infected ulcer and limb threat in poorly perfused diabetic feet within days.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet: olive oil, fish, legumes, vegetables, and whole grains — reduces cardiovascular events by 30% and slows atherosclerosis progression
Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) 2-3 times per week — omega-3 fatty acids reduce triglycerides and systemic inflammation
Soluble fibre (oats, psyllium, barley, lentils) — lowers LDL cholesterol by 5-7% at 10 g/day
Low-sodium diet below 2,300 mg/day to support blood pressure control
avoid: Trans fats and partially hydrogenated oils — ban in processed foods is patchy; read labels on pastries, fried foods, and margarine
Saturated fat above 7% of daily calories — limit red meat and full-fat dairy
Excess sodium from processed meats, canned soups, and restaurant meals
High-glycaemic index carbohydrates (white bread, sugary drinks) that worsen insulin resistance and glucose control in diabetic PAD patients

exerciseGuidelines: Supervised exercise rehabilitation — 30-60 minutes of intermittent walking to moderate claudication pain, 3 times per week for at least 12 weeks — is the most effective non-surgical treatment for claudication. Community-walking and home-based programmes are alternatives when supervised programmes are unavailable, though with somewhat lower efficacy. Patients should not walk to tissue-damaging ischaemic levels and should rest before pain becomes severe. After revascularisation, graduated walking increases graft function and functional capacity; formal cardiac rehabilitation programmes accept PAD patients in many centres.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed medications (statin, antiplatelet, ACE inhibitor) at the same time each day.
Walk for at least 30 minutes daily — structured walking is both preventive and therapeutic for claudication; use a walking diary to track progress.
Inspect feet each morning: look for redness, blisters, broken skin, or temperature change; report any wound to your clinical team within 24-48 hours.
Measure blood pressure at home weekly; target below 130/80 mmHg.
Attend all vascular surveillance appointments; duplex ultrasound graft checks at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and annually.
Never sit with legs crossed for extended periods — reduces already-compromised arterial inflow.
prognosis: Prognosis in PAD has two dimensions: limb prognosis and systemic cardiovascular prognosis. For claudication managed medically and with exercise, approximately 70-75% of patients remain stable or improve over 5 years with modern guideline-directed therapy; only 1-2% per year progress to critical limb ischaemia. However, cardiovascular prognosis is poor: 5-year cardiovascular mortality in PAD is 15-30%, reflecting the polyvascular nature of atherosclerosis. For critical limb-threatening ischaemia, 1-year outcomes are sobering — approximately 25% mortality, 30% major amputation, and only 45% alive with both limbs — the so-called dismal triad. Successful revascularisation for CLTI (either endovascular or bypass) improves 1-year limb salvage to 80-85%. Predictors of poor outcome include chronic kidney disease, diabetes, non-healing wound with deep infection, and failure of two or more prior revascularisation attempts.
recoveryTimeline: After endovascular angioplasty/stenting: hospital 1-2 days; walking rehabilitation begins same day; return to full activity in 1-2 weeks; duplex graft check at 6 weeks. After bypass surgery: hospital 5-7 days; wound healing 4-6 weeks; supervised walking programme begins at 6-8 weeks; full return to activity 8-12 weeks. After major amputation: wound healing 4-8 weeks; prosthetic casting at 4-6 weeks; learning to walk with prosthesis 3-6 months; full rehabilitation 6-12 months.
complications: Critical limb-threatening ischaemia — progression from claudication in 1-2% per year without adequate treatment; presents with rest pain and tissue loss; early vascular referral dramatically reduces amputation rates.
Major amputation — occurs in approximately 30% of CLTI patients without revascularisation; below-knee amputation preserves knee and allows better prosthetic rehabilitation than above-knee.
Graft or stent restenosis — occurs in 20-40% of femoropopliteal procedures within 2 years; duplex ultrasound surveillance every 6 months detects stenosis before occlusion.
Cardiovascular events — MI, stroke, and cardiovascular death are the dominant causes of mortality in PAD; event rates 3-5× higher than age-matched controls without PAD.
Wound infection and foot sepsis in diabetic PAD — can progress to gas gangrene within days from minor wound; immediate wound-care and vascular assessment are essential at first presentation.
Compartment syndrome after acute limb ischaemia reperfusion — massive swelling of the calf compartments after restoration of flow to ischaemic muscle; treated with urgent four-compartment fasciotomy.

supportResources.name: Vascular Society of Great Britain & Ireland — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Clear patient-facing information on PAD symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options including angioplasty and bypass surgery.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Peripheral Artery Disease
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient resources on PAD risk factors, ABI testing, lifestyle management, and finding a vascular specialist.

supportResources.name: Society for Vascular Surgery — Patient Hub
supportResources.description: US vascular surgery professional society patient resources on PAD diagnosis, claudication, CLTI, and limb salvage options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sciatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatica (lumbosacral nerve root compression) causes leg pain and paraesthesia following a dermatomal distribution, worsens with sitting and spinal flexion, and is NOT reliably triggered by walking distance or relieved immediately by standing still. Claudication from PAD is reproducibly triggered by a fixed walking distance and resolves within 5 minutes of rest, independent of posture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral Neuropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral neuropathy causes burning, tingling, and numbness in a stocking-glove distribution, present at rest and often worse at night. Claudication is an exercise-induced cramp or fatigue that resolves rapidly with rest. Both can coexist in diabetics — ankle-brachial index and nerve conduction studies distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hip or Knee Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoarthritis causes joint pain that is worsened by weight-bearing and may be relieved by rest, but is typically also triggered by stairs, rising from chairs, and rotational movements — and does not resolve reliably within 5 minutes of simply standing. Claudication is purely arterial and resolves with brief rest regardless of posture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis / Phlebitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DVT causes calf swelling, redness, warmth, and tenderness — a venous, not arterial, presentation. ABI is normal in DVT. PAD causes pale, cold extremities with reduced pulses. DVT is diagnosed by venous duplex ultrasound; PAD by ABI and arterial duplex.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: PAD and CAD share the same atherosclerotic substrate; 40-60% of PAD patients have concurrent significant coronary disease.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: All three (PAD, CAD, stroke) are manifestations of systemic atherosclerosis; PAD doubles the risk of concurrent cerebrovascular disease.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: CKD triples PAD prevalence and markedly worsens CLTI outcomes; calcific arteriopathy in uraemia limits ABI accuracy and revascularisation options.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes triples PAD risk, produces a distal vessel distribution, and dramatically increases the risk of foot ulceration and amputation.

faqs.question: What is peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: Peripheral artery disease is atherosclerotic narrowing of the arteries that supply the legs, caused by the same cholesterol plaque build-up that causes heart attacks. It reduces blood flow to the muscles and, in severe cases, to the skin and foot, causing leg pain while walking, non-healing wounds, and risk of amputation.

faqs.question: What does PAD leg pain feel like?
faqs.answer: PAD causes intermittent claudication — a reproducible cramping, aching, or heavy feeling in the calf, thigh, or buttock that comes on at a consistent walking distance and disappears within 5 minutes of resting. It is not felt at rest in mild disease. Severe PAD causes constant burning pain in the foot even at rest.

faqs.question: How is peripheral artery disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is the key diagnostic test: a ratio of blood pressure at the ankle to the arm below 0.9 confirms PAD. It is painless, takes 15-20 minutes, and uses a Doppler probe. Duplex ultrasound and CT angiography then map the location and severity of stenoses before treatment planning.

faqs.question: Can walking cure peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: Supervised exercise rehabilitation — walking to near-maximum pain three times per week for at least 12 weeks — improves claudication walking distance by 100-180% and is a Class I guideline recommendation. It does not remove plaque but builds collateral circulation. It is the most effective non-surgical treatment for claudication.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: All patients need guideline-directed medical therapy: high-intensity statin, aspirin or clopidogrel, blood pressure control, and smoking cessation. Supervised exercise is added for claudication. When claudication is disabling despite 3 months of exercise, endovascular angioplasty or stenting is considered. Critical limb ischaemia requires urgent revascularisation.

faqs.question: Is peripheral artery disease the same as peripheral vascular disease?
faqs.answer: Peripheral vascular disease is a broader term covering any blood vessel disease outside the heart and brain, including venous disease. Peripheral artery disease refers specifically to arterial obstruction — most commonly from atherosclerosis. PAD is the most common form of peripheral vascular disease.

faqs.question: Can peripheral artery disease cause amputation?
faqs.answer: Yes, in severe cases. Critical limb-threatening ischaemia (rest pain, ulcers, or gangrene) carries a 30% major amputation risk within 1 year without revascularisation. With successful bypass or endovascular surgery, limb salvage exceeds 80% at 1 year. Early diagnosis and treatment dramatically reduce amputation risk.

faqs.question: What is an ankle-brachial index (ABI) test?
faqs.answer: The ABI test compares blood pressure at the ankle with blood pressure at the arm using a Doppler device. A ratio below 0.9 indicates arterial narrowing consistent with PAD. The test takes 15-20 minutes, requires no needles or radiation, and is performed in most vascular clinics or GP surgeries with Doppler equipment.

faqs.question: Does smoking cause peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: Yes, smoking is the strongest single modifiable risk factor for PAD, conferring 2-4 times the risk of non-smokers. Duration and pack-years are dose-dependent. Smokers with PAD develop disease earlier, progress faster, and have worse outcomes after intervention. Quitting smoking is the most effective treatment available.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: Diabetes triples or quadruples the risk of PAD and changes its pattern — affecting small tibial arteries rather than larger vessels, causing more foot ulcers and amputations. Every 1% rise in HbA1c increases PAD risk by 28%. Daily foot inspection and glycaemic control are the cornerstones of PAD prevention in diabetes.

faqs.question: What is critical limb ischaemia?
faqs.answer: Critical limb-threatening ischaemia (CLTI) is severe PAD where resting blood flow is insufficient to sustain tissue viability. It presents as rest pain in the foot, non-healing arterial ulcers, or gangrene. Without urgent revascularisation, approximately 30% of patients lose a limb within 1 year and 25% die from cardiovascular causes.

faqs.question: What is the difference between claudication and peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: Claudication is the main symptom of PAD — leg pain triggered by walking that resolves with rest. PAD is the underlying arterial disease. About 50% of PAD patients have no claudication but still have a low ABI and elevated cardiovascular risk. Claudication is therefore a sign, and PAD is the diagnosis confirmed by ABI testing.

faqs.question: How long can you live with peripheral artery disease?
faqs.answer: PAD significantly reduces life expectancy: 5-year cardiovascular mortality is 15-30%, reflecting widespread systemic atherosclerosis. With modern guideline-directed therapy — statins, antiplatelet agents, ACE inhibitors, smoking cessation, and exercise — event rates are substantially reduced. The leading cause of death is heart attack or stroke, not limb loss.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PAD stenting and bypass surgery?
faqs.answer: Stenting uses a catheter to insert a metal scaffold inside the narrowed artery from inside the vessel, via a small puncture in the groin. Bypass surgery creates a detour using a vein or artificial graft sewn around the blockage through an open incision. Stenting has faster recovery; bypass tends to have better long-term patency for complex tibial disease.

faqs.question: Can peripheral artery disease affect the arms?
faqs.answer: Yes, though far less commonly than the legs. Upper-limb PAD most often affects the subclavian or axillary artery. It causes arm fatigue during use and, in severe cases, hand ischaemia. Subclavian artery disease can cause subclavian steal syndrome, where blood is diverted from the brain during arm exercise, causing dizziness.

simpleName: Peripheral artery disease (PAD, peripheral vascular disease, claudication)
symptomKeywords: calf pain walking stops with rest
metaTitle: Peripheral Artery Disease: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Peripheral artery disease narrows leg arteries causing claudication, rest pain, and ulcers. ABI diagnosis, statin therapy, stenting and bypass surgery reviewed.
